<img src="https://r2cdn.perplexity.ai/pplx-full-logo-primary-dark%402x.png" style="height:64px;margin-right:32px"/>

# Apply a priority matrix to the ideas here to identify likely impactful designs that are lower in complexity and require lower resources to implement successfully.

Are there ideas that work well together?

Here is the full priority analysis. The matrix above gives the spatial picture — the written analysis below is where the real value sits.

***

![](data:image/png;base64,)

## The Quick Wins Quadrant

These are high-impact, low-complexity ideas that should be in the first build. Taken together they represent a meaningful upgrade from the baseline wizard design with minimal additional development.

**Assertion cards instead of questions** is the single highest-priority idea in the entire set. Rewriting every question as a system claim (*"We think this goes to clients"* instead of *"Who receives the output?"*) is a copy change with a UI interaction change — no new backend, no new schema. The cognitive impact is disproportionate to the effort.

**Session warmth indicators** and **animated progress (understanding-based)** are both pure copy changes. Zero engineering cost. The difference between *"Step 4 of 8"* and *"We understand the task, the audience, and the timing — still building: difficulty, concerns"* is the difference between filling a form and briefing a team.

**Done for now exit** is standard session-persistence logic that most web frameworks handle natively. The UX impact — removing the anxiety of commitment — is high because Jordan is a business owner doing this between other tasks, not a power user in a dedicated session.

**Confidence indicators on inferences** requires a small badge or fill bar added to each inference card. The backend already calculates confidence implicitly — making it visible to Jordan transforms the trust dynamic from *"trust us"* to *"check our working."*

**The design brief metaphor** is a zero-cost reframing. Change the opening copy from *"Design your agent workflow"* to *"Brief your agent team"* and the mental model shifts immediately. Jordan already knows how to brief a team. The metaphor unlocks familiarity rather than demanding new learning.

***

## The Strategic Bets Quadrant

These are high-impact but require meaningful investment. They should be planned for but not required in the first build.

**Confidence-first / edit-down paradigm** is the most transformative idea in the set — and the most expensive to implement properly. It requires the system to produce a complete draft analysis from the raw description alone before any structured input, then present that draft for correction rather than asking questions to build toward it. The first build needs clarification questions because the models aren't good enough yet to produce reliable drafts without structured input. But this is the direction of travel.

**Task archetype matching** and **template-first, difference-only** work as a pair and together represent the fastest path to value for returning users. A library of 8–12 well-designed archetypes (weekly report, client communication, approval workflow, complaint handling, data aggregation) would reduce the median session from 12 minutes to under 4. The investment is in building and maintaining the archetype library, not in the UI.

**Inline consequence preview** has high impact and medium complexity — it requires the system to interpret each selection in real time and generate a plain-language consequence statement. This is a small LLM call per answer or a rule-based lookup. Worth prioritising in the second build.

**Live human/agent ratio indicator** requires the scoring model to run continuously as answers come in, not just at the end in Stage 5. Technically achievable but requires the property scoring logic to be available client-side and fast enough to update on each interaction.

***

## Fill-Ins

Low complexity, moderate impact. Include them when convenient but don't prioritise.

- **Keyboard navigation** — accessibility standard, rarely used by Jordan but raises the quality floor
- **Drag to prioritise** — available as a standard UI library component; adds interest to the concerns screen without changing the data model
- **Friction-free annotation** — a note icon on any card opens a text field; low dev, useful for the subset of users who want to capture nuance

***

## Ideas That Work Well Together

Five clusters with natural affinity — ideas that share a design logic and reinforce each other:

### Cluster A: Show Me, I'll Correct It

*Assertion cards + Confidence-first paradigm + Outcome-led validation*

These three share one underlying model: the system does the work, the user audits it. Deploy assertion cards in the first build as a low-cost version of this paradigm. As model quality improves, replace the structured question flow with a full draft-first approach. Outcome-led validation is the mid-point between the two — the system proposes outcomes, Jordan validates them, rather than building them through questions. These three ideas form a roadmap, not competing alternatives.

### Cluster B: Live Understanding

*Inline consequence preview + Live H/A ratio indicator + Real-time risk counter + Task profile card + This will matter later marker*

These all share the same design commitment: Jordan should never have to wait until the end to understand what their answers mean. Each idea adds a signal to a different part of the interface. The consequence preview tells Jordan what a specific answer means. The H/A ratio indicator shows the aggregate effect. The risk counter tracks accumulating concern. The task profile card synthesises all of it. Deploy them in sequence — the consequence preview first (highest impact), then the profile card, then the ratio indicator as the scoring model matures.

### Cluster C: Low Friction and Autonomy

*Abbreviated fast-track + Done for now exit + Skip ahead button + Undo stack*

These share the philosophy that Jordan's time is the scarcest resource in the system. The done-for-now exit and abbreviated fast-track are the most important — they address the single biggest drop-off risk (commitment anxiety and time pressure). The skip ahead button and undo stack are enhancements for confident returning users. Deploy the exit in the first build, the fast-track in the second.

### Cluster D: Context-First Entry

*Task archetype matching + Template-first flow + Design brief metaphor + Import and extract*

These all move the session start from zero to something. The metaphor is free. The archetype gallery is the first build of the template system. Import and extract is the most powerful but most complex. The progression is: metaphor first → archetypes next → templates → extract. Each builds on the previous without requiring it.

### Cluster E: Trust Architecture

*Confidence indicators + Show your working toggle + Session warmth indicators + Friction-free annotation*

These all address Jordan's underlying anxiety: *"I don't know if I can trust this."* None of them change the analysis. All of them change how Jordan relates to it. They cost almost nothing individually. Deployed together they create a consistent signal throughout the session that the system is transparent, the reasoning is inspectable, and Jordan's contributions are noticed.

***

## The Recommended First Build Upgrade Set

Applied to the current wireframe design, these seven ideas require the least effort and deliver the most meaningful experience improvement over the baseline:


| Idea | Effort | Primary effect |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Assertion cards instead of questions | Copy + interaction | Cognitive ease; ownership |
| Design brief metaphor | Copy only | Familiarity; framing |
| Animated progress (understanding-based) | Copy only | Reassurance; engagement |
| Session warmth indicators | Copy only | Trust; acknowledgment |
| Confidence indicators on inferences | Small UI badge | Transparency |
| Done for now exit | Session persistence | Reduces commitment anxiety |
| This will matter later marker | Small UI label | Narrative continuity |

Together these seven ideas cost less than one sprint to implement and move the experience from *competent B2B software* to something that feels like it was designed specifically for how Jordan thinks, works, and worries.
